claimed_contribution: The paper argues and shows that proactively revealing seller cost at the start of negotiation can build trust and improve profits, challenging the standard view that revealing reservation-price information is always harmful in bargaining.

It introduces a two-part negotiation perspective by linking a relatively information-symmetric front end to an information-asymmetric aftermarket back end, and studies how front-end behavior affects back-end outcomes.
It contributes to personal selling theory by showing that early cost disclosure can function as both a relational and a transactional selling behavior.
context: The focal empirical setting is automobile retailing, where buyers negotiate vehicle price first and then face a finance-and-insurance aftermarket with add-ons, warranties, and related services. The paper positions this as representative of negotiated markets in which front-end information has become widely available online while back-end information remains more asymmetric.
design_and_data: The authors run five studies: one observational field study and four experiments. Study 1 observes 400 successful buyer-seller negotiations in a large U.S. dealership chain, combines observer and salesperson survey measures with dealership CRM profit data (front end and F&I back end), and tracks service outcomes up to one year later; treatment categories are early, late, reactive, and no cost disclosure. The field analysis reports OLS plus robustness checks using multivalued propensity-score weighting and multinomial selection modeling. Studies 2-4 are controlled experiments (lab negotiation and scenario designs; n=480, n=586, n=700) that manipulate disclosure timing and information/verifiability (and channel in Study 2), and Study 5 in the Web Appendix provides an additional scenario replication with an alternative true-value operationalization.
main_findings: In the field study, early cost disclosure is associated with substantially higher back-end profits (about $1,400 more on average than late/no disclosure) and higher total profits, while front-end profits are not significantly lower than in the no-disclosure condition.

Across experiments, early disclosure increases back-end spending/profits relative to late or no disclosure only when customers can verify front-end cost information (informed or verifiable conditions), not when customers are uninformed.

Trust mediates the effect of early disclosure on back-end outcomes, including a sequential path from trust in the salesperson to trust in the seller, and the effect is weaker in online negotiations than in face-to-face negotiations.
one_line_summary: The paper shows that revealing seller cost at the beginning of a negotiation can increase back-end aftermarket profits by building verifiable trust, especially in face-to-face settings and when customers can verify the disclosed information.
relation_to_prior_work: extends bargaining and negotiation research that, under information asymmetry, generally predicts sellers should not reveal reservation-price information and treats information sharing mainly as a consequence of trust. It also extends front-end-focused negotiation work by showing that disclosure timing and front-end verifiability shape downstream aftermarket profitability.
research_gap: Prior negotiation research largely assumes information asymmetry, emphasizes front-end negotiated outcomes, and rarely examines explicit disclosure of sensitive value information such as seller cost. It has not examined two-part negotiations where front-end information is symmetric but back-end information remains asymmetric, nor how disclosure timing across stages affects trust and back-end profits.
research_question: Can salespeople improve back-end aftermarket profitability by proactively disclosing seller cost at the beginning of the front-end negotiation? Does this effect operate through customer trust, and does it depend on customers' ability to verify the disclosed information and on negotiation channel (face-to-face vs online)?
